county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1855 from unorganized territory in northwest Lower Michigan. County seat is Manistee on Lake Michigan. Named from Ojibwe for 'spirit of the woods.' Major 19th-century lumber port.
county_metadata.research_tips: Manistee County Register of Deeds in Manistee holds land records from 1855 organization. County Clerk has marriage records and court files. The Manistee County Historical Museum has local genealogical materials. Manistee was one of Michigan's premier lumber towns on Lake Michigan -- sawmill records and shipping manifests are valuable. Danish, Swedish, Polish, and German immigrants settled in large numbers. Catholic and Lutheran church records document immigrant families. The Manistee News Advocate newspaper archives from the 1860s. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Archives of Michigan has lumber company records.

source_profiles.source_name: Manistee County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1855 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

source_profiles.source_name: Manistee County Probate Records -- Probate Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estate inventories, guardianship records, and administration files from 1855. Michigan Probate Courts also handled adoption records, mental health commitments, and name changes.

source_profiles.source_name: Manistee County Marriage Records -- County Clerk
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1855. Michigan County Clerks issue marriage licenses. State registration of marriages began 1867. Pre-1867 marriages may also be documented in church records.

source_profiles.source_name: Manistee County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Manistee County from 1860 to 1950. Michigan territorial censuses (1827, 1834) may include early residents. Michigan state censuses (1874, 1884, 1894, 1904) provide intercensal data.

source_profiles.source_name: Manistee County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Michigan is a public land state. Original federal land patents for Manistee County are available free online at BLM GLO. Cash entry, homestead, and military bounty land warrants. Essential for identifying first landowners.

source_profiles.source_name: Ottawa/Odawa Tribal Records -- Manistee County Area
source_profiles.repository: NARA (Great Lakes Agency) / Tribal Government Offices / Archives of Michigan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Treaty records, tribal census rolls (annuity rolls), allotment records, and agency correspondence for Ottawa/Odawa communities in Manistee County. NARA holds Bureau of Indian Affairs records (Great Lakes Agency). Tribal government offices maintain enrollment records. Archives of Michigan has related territorial records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1843 from unorganized territory in the Upper Peninsula. County seat is Marquette. Named for Jacques Marquette, French missionary-explorer. Largest city in the UP. Iron mining heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: Marquette County Register of Deeds in Marquette holds land records from 1843 -- one of the earliest Upper Peninsula counties. County Clerk maintains marriage records and court files. The Marquette Regional History Center has outstanding genealogical collections including mining records, immigration files, and ethnic community documentation. Iron mining (Marquette Iron Range) was the dominant industry. The Peter White Public Library in Marquette has genealogy resources. Northern Michigan University archives hold local materials. Finnish, Swedish, Italian, Cornish, French-Canadian, and Irish miners settled in large numbers. Michigan Technological University Archives has mining company records. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. Catholic and Lutheran church records essential.

source_profiles.source_name: Marquette County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1843 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

source_profiles.source_name: Marquette County Probate Records -- Probate Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estate inventories, guardianship records, and administration files from 1843. Michigan Probate Courts also handled adoption records, mental health commitments, and name changes.

source_profiles.source_name: Marquette County Marriage Records -- County Clerk
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1843. Michigan County Clerks issue marriage licenses. State registration of marriages began 1867. Pre-1867 marriages may also be documented in church records.

source_profiles.source_name: Marquette County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Marquette County from 1850 to 1950. Michigan territorial censuses (1827, 1834) may include early residents. Michigan state censuses (1874, 1884, 1894, 1904) provide intercensal data.

source_profiles.source_name: Marquette County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Michigan is a public land state. Original federal land patents for Marquette County are available free online at BLM GLO. Cash entry, homestead, and military bounty land warrants. Essential for identifying first landowners.

source_profiles.source_name: Marquette County Mining Company Records
source_profiles.repository: Michigan Technological University Archives / Archives of Michigan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Employment rolls, payroll records, accident reports, and company town records from iron mining operations in Marquette County. Michigan Tech's Copper Country Historical Collections and the Archives of Michigan hold mining company records. Essential for tracing immigrant mining families.

source_profiles.source_name: Marquette County Historical Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Archives of Michigan / Library of Congress / Newspapers.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers for Marquette County including local weeklies and dailies. Obituaries, legal notices, marriage and death announcements, estate settlements. Michigan Digital Newspaper Archive and Chronicling America have digitized collections.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1855 from unorganized territory in west-central Lower Michigan. County seat is Ludington on Lake Michigan. Named for Stevens T. Mason, Michigan's first governor. Lumber port and car ferry terminus.
county_metadata.research_tips: Mason County Register of Deeds in Ludington holds land records from 1855 organization. County Clerk has marriage records and court files. The Mason County Historical Society and Rose Hawley Museum have local genealogical materials. Ludington was a major lumber port on Lake Michigan and later a car ferry terminus. Scandinavian (especially Swedish) immigrants settled in significant numbers. Pere Marquette Railroad records document workers and transportation. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Archives of Michigan has supplementary records. Ludington Daily News archives.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1855 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Michigan is a public land state. Original federal land patents for Mason County are available free online at BLM GLO. Cash entry, homestead, and military bounty land warrants. Essential for identifying first landowners.

source_profiles.repository: NARA (Great Lakes Agency) / Tribal Government Offices / Archives of Michigan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Treaty records, tribal census rolls (annuity rolls), allotment records, and agency correspondence for Ottawa/Odawa communities in Mason County. NARA holds Bureau of Indian Affairs records (Great Lakes Agency). Tribal government offices maintain enrollment records. Archives of Michigan has related territorial records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1859 from unorganized territory in west-central Lower Michigan. County seat is Big Rapids. Named for a Potawatomi chief. Home to Ferris State University. Lumber heritage on the Muskegon River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Mecosta County Register of Deeds in Big Rapids holds land records from 1859 organization. County Clerk has marriage records and court files. Probate Court has estate files. The Mecosta County Genealogical Society has compiled family files and cemetery records. Big Rapids was a lumber town on the Muskegon River. Ferris State University in Big Rapids has local history archives. Irish, German, and Canadian settlers formed early communities. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Archives of Michigan has lumber company records relevant to the Muskegon River watershed.

source_profiles.source_name: Mecosta County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1859 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Michigan is a public land state. Original federal land patents for Mecosta County are available free online at BLM GLO. Cash entry, homestead, and military bounty land warrants. Essential for identifying first landowners.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions for Mecosta County churches, township, and family burial grounds. Find A Grave has growing online coverage. Local historical and genealogical societies have published cemetery surveys. Michigan Cemetery Source at the Library of Michigan is a statewide index.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1863 from unorganized territory in the southwestern Upper Peninsula. County seat is Menominee on Green Bay. Named for the Menominee people. Lumber port on the Wisconsin border.
county_metadata.research_tips: Menominee County Register of Deeds in Menominee holds land records from 1863 organization. County Clerk has marriage records and court files. The Menominee County Historical Society has local materials. Lumber industry and later iron mining drove settlement. The city of Menominee on Green Bay was a major lumber port. French-Canadian, Swedish, Finnish, and German immigrants predominated. Catholic and Lutheran church records are essential. Check the Archives of Michigan and Michigan Technological University for UP industrial records. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. Wisconsin border -- some families straddled the state line (check Marinette County, WI for related records).

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1863 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

Michigan Tech's Copper Country Historical Collections and the Archives of Michigan hold mining company records. Essential for tracing immigrant mining families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1850 from Saginaw County in east-central Lower Michigan. County seat is Midland. Named for its central location. Home to Dow Chemical Company (1897) and the Dow Gardens.
county_metadata.research_tips: Midland County Register of Deeds in Midland holds land records from 1850 organization. County Clerk maintains marriage licenses and court records. Probate Court has estate files. The Midland County Historical Society has genealogical materials. The Grace A. Dow Memorial Library has local history and genealogy collections. Dow Chemical Company (founded 1897) records document Midland-area workers and families. Lumber industry preceded chemical manufacturing. German settlers were numerous. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. The Archives of Michigan has supplementary records. Midland Daily News archives.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1850 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1871 from unorganized territory in north-central Lower Michigan. County seat is Lake City. Named for a Potawatomi chief. Lumber heritage county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Missaukee County Register of Deeds in Lake City holds land records from 1871 organization. County Clerk has marriage records and court files. The Missaukee County Historical Society has local materials. Lumber industry was the primary economic driver. Danish immigrants formed a notable community. The county was sparsely settled after timber was exhausted. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Archives of Michigan has supplementary records. Lake City newspapers document local events.

source_profiles.source_name: Missaukee County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1817 as one of Michigan Territory's earliest organized counties from Wayne. County seat is Monroe. Named for President James Monroe. Site of the Battle of the River Raisin (1813). French-Canadian heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: Monroe County Register of Deeds in Monroe holds land records from 1817 -- one of Michigan's earliest organized counties. County Clerk maintains marriage records from the 1820s and court records. Probate Court has estate files from the territorial period. The Monroe County Museum has genealogical collections. French-Canadian families settled along the River Raisin from the 1780s. The Battle of the River Raisin (1813, War of 1812) records at NARA and Archives of Michigan. Monroe County was part of the Toledo War dispute with Ohio. Catholic parish records (St. Mary and St. Antoine) from the French colonial era. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Detroit Society for Genealogical Research has French-Canadian resources.

source_profiles.source_name: Monroe County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1818 to present. Michigan is a public land state -- original federal land patents available through BLM GLO. Register of Deeds holds grantor/grantee indexes, deed books, mortgages, and liens.

source_profiles.source_name: Monroe County Probate Records -- Probate Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estate inventories, guardianship records, and administration files from 1818. Michigan Probate Courts also handled adoption records, mental health commitments, and name changes.

source_profiles.source_name: Monroe County Marriage Records -- County Clerk
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1818. Michigan County Clerks issue marriage licenses. State registration of marriages began 1867. Pre-1867 marriages may also be documented in church records.

source_profiles.source_name: Monroe County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Monroe County from 1827 to 1950. Michigan territorial censuses (1827, 1834) may include early residents. Michigan state censuses (1874, 1884, 1894, 1904) provide intercensal data.

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Michigan is a public land state. Original federal land patents for Monroe County are available free online at BLM GLO. Cash entry, homestead, and military bounty land warrants. Essential for identifying first landowners.

source_profiles.source_name: Monroe County French Colonial and Catholic Parish Records
source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Detroit Archives / Archives of Michigan / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish records including baptisms, marriages, and burials.  French-Canadian families from the fur trade era are well documented in Catholic registers. FamilySearch has microfilmed many Michigan Catholic parish records.

source_profiles.source_name: Monroe County Tax Rolls -- Archives of Michigan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Annual tax rolls listing property owners, assessed values, and personal property. Michigan tax rolls from 1818 at the Archives of Michigan. Essential for tracking land ownership and residency between census years.

source_profiles.source_name: Monroe County Historical Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Archives of Michigan / Library of Congress / Newspapers.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers for Monroe County including local weeklies and dailies. Obituaries, legal notices, marriage and death announcements, estate settlements. Michigan Digital Newspaper Archive and Chronicling America have digitized collections.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1850 from unorganized territory in central Lower Michigan. County seat is Stanton. Named for French General Louis-Joseph de Montcalm. Agricultural county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Montcalm County Register of Deeds in Stanton holds land records from 1850 organization. County Clerk has marriage records and court files. Probate Court has estate files. The Montcalm County Historical Society and Montcalm Community College library have local history materials. Settlers came from New York, New England, and Ohio. Danish immigrants settled near Gowen and Greenville. Lumber and agriculture were the primary industries. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Archives of Michigan has supplementary records. The Flat River Historical Museum in Greenville has local materials.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1881 from unorganized territory in northeast Lower Michigan. County seat is Atlanta (Michigan). Named for a Schoolcraft coinage. Sparsely populated; elk reintroduction area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Montmorency County Register of Deeds in Atlanta holds land records from 1881 organization. County Clerk has marriage records and court files. The Montmorency County Historical Society in Atlanta (Michigan) has local materials. Lumber industry drove early settlement. The Thunder Bay River watershed was a major log-driving area. The county is one of Michigan's most sparsely populated. FamilySearch microfilm covers vital records. BLM GLO land patents. Archives of Michigan has lumber company records. Elk hunting heritage -- game records document seasonal residents.
